claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 8 years old and does not live with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 8 (under 16)
  - Claimant contributes £30.00/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 8 (under 16); Claimant contributes £30.00/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Claimant contributes £30.00/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. To assess your eligibility, I need to ask you a few questions.

Let's start with: **Do you have any children, and if so, how many children are you inquiring about?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I have one child - my son Alex. He's 8 years old. I'd like to know if I can claim Child Benefit for him.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! I can see you have one child, Alex, who is 8 years old.

To assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex, I need to ask you a few questions.

First: **Do you currently live in the UK?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I do live in the UK.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great, thank you. 

Next question: **Does Alex live with you?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he doesn't live with me.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for letting me know. Since Alex doesn't live with you, I need to understand your situation a bit more.

**Are you paying towards Alex's upkeep (such as for food, clothes, or pocket money), and if so, how much do you contribute?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I do help out with his upkeep. I pay for things like his clothes, school dinners, and bits and bobs he needs. I contribute about £30 a week.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. It's helpful to know you're contributing £30 a week towards Alex's upkeep.

**Who does Alex currently live with?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: He lives with his mum, my ex-partner.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that Alex lives with his mum, your ex-partner.

**Is his mum currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, she's not claiming Child Benefit for him.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. I have a few more questions to help determine your eligibility.

**How long has Alex been living with his mum (not with you)?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: He's been living with her for quite a while now - a few years actually. It's been the arrangement since we separated.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. I have enough details now to assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex.

Let me carefully consider your situation based on the rules:

- You live in the UK ✓
- Alex is 8 years old (under 16) ✓
- Alex lives with his mum, not with you
- You contribute £30 per week towards his upkeep
- His mum is not currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex
- This arrangement has been in place for a few years

According to the rules, you're normally responsible for a child if you either live with them OR you're paying at least the same amount as Child Benefit towards looking after them.

The current Child Benefit rate is £25.60 per week for the eldest/only child. Since you're contributing £30 per week towards Alex's upkeep (which includes clothes, school dinners, and other necessities), this exceeds the Child Benefit amount.

This means you could potentially qualify for Child Benefit for Alex, even though he doesn't live with you, because you're making sufficient contributions to his upkeep.

However, it's important to note that only one person can claim Child Benefit for a child. Since Alex's mum isn't claiming, you would be able to make a claim. If she decides to claim in the future, you would need to agree between yourselves who should receive it.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Good news! You are eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex.**

Based on your circumstances:
- You live in the UK and Alex is 8 years old (under 16)
- Even though Alex doesn't live with you, you're contributing £30 per week towards his upkeep
- This exceeds the current Child Benefit rate of £25.60 per week for an eldest/only child
- Alex's mum is not currently claiming Child Benefit

This means you meet the criteria for being "responsible" for Alex under the Child Benefit rules, and you can make a claim.

**Important things to remember:**
- You should keep records of your contributions to Alex's upkeep
- If Alex's mum decides to claim Child Benefit in the future, only one of you can receive it, so you'd need to agree who claims
- If you or your partner have an individual income over £60,000, you may have to pay a High Income Child Benefit tax charge

Would you like information on how to make your claim?

According to the rules, you're usually responsible for a child if you're paying at least the same amount as Child Benefit towards looking after them. The current Child Benefit rate for an eldest/only child is £25.60 per week, and the claimant's contribution of £30 per week exceeds this amount. Additionally, Alex's mother (who he lives with) is not currently claiming Child Benefit for him. Therefore, the claimant is eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex.
